# Sub-prompt 14 — Anxiety-Inoculation Feature: App Design, Falsifiable Ablation, and the Feature Decision

**Bottom line:** Anxiety inoculation should **COMPLEMENT interleaving, not replace it, and should NOT consume the single learning-science "feature + ablation" slot.** The test-anxiety→performance link is real but modest (von der Embse et al. 2018 synthesized 238 studies and found test anxiety "negatively related to a range of educational performance outcomes," with the cognitive/worry facet most predictive), yet the evidence that brief, app-deliverable interventions *raise objective scores* is weak and fragile: the flagship classroom finding (Ramirez & Beilock 2011) failed two well-powered replications (Camerer et al. 2018), a 2026 multi-institution reappraisal replication found null effects on both anxiety and performance (Thormodsæter et al. 2026), and the best meta-analytic estimate for performance gains is only g ≈ 0.28 (Huntley et al. 2019, with outliers removed). The defensible move is to ship the cheap components (arousal-reappraisal microcopy + optional worry-dump) as un-ablated defaults and justify an "exam-pressure simulator" on construct-coverage grounds (GRE speededness), while keeping interleaving as the pre-registered ablation.

## TL;DR
- The credible win from test-anxiety interventions on **objective scores** is small, narrow, and replication-fragile (Huntley et al. 2019: performance g = 0.37 overall, dropping to g = 0.28 with outliers removed; flagship studies failed to replicate), so it is too thin to justify displacing interleaving.
- Build the feature anyway as a **low-cost complement**: reappraisal microcopy + a 2–3 minute worry-dump + an exam-pressure simulator (authentic timing/no-pause), with the simulator justified by the speededness construct rather than the anxiety literature.
- At tiny n the honest deliverable is **the pre-registered design plus a TOST equivalence/estimation result**, not a significant effect; the recommendation flips to "co-primary" only if the high-trait-anxious subgroup shows an under-pressure score benefit beyond the equivalence bound.

## Key Findings
- **Test anxiety reliably predicts lower performance, but modestly and mostly via the cognitive ("worry") component.** von der Embse et al. (2018, *J. Affective Disorders* 227:483–493) synthesized "Results of 238 studies from 1988 to the present"; key findings: "Test anxiety was negatively related to a range of educational performance outcomes," "Test anxiety effects were most pronounced at the middle grades level," and "Self-esteem was a significant and strong predictor of test anxiety." Secondary syntheses place the typical correlation in the small-to-medium range (rs roughly −.13 to −.40 across formats), with the cognitive facet more predictive than emotionality (consistent with Cassady & Johnson 2002 and Hembree 1988). [objective-score + self-report correlational; meta-analysis; high confidence]
- **Anxiety-*reduction* effects are medium; performance effects are weak.** Ergene (2003, *School Psychology International* 24(3):313–328), 56 studies (n = 2,482): "the overall mean effect size (ES) for test anxiety reduction programs was E++ = 0.65 (95 percent confidence intervals [CI] +0.58 to 0.73)"; academic performance was not examined. Huntley et al. (2019, *J. Anxiety Disorders* 63:36–50): "Forty-four RCTs met our eligibility criteria (n = 2,209). Interventions were superior to control conditions at post-treatment for reducing TA (g = −0.76) and improving academic performance (g = 0.37)"; with outlier studies removed these dropped to **g = −0.64 (anxiety) and g = 0.28 (performance)**. The asymmetry is the crux: you can reliably move *how anxious people feel* but barely move *what they score*. [self-report vs objective-score distinguished; meta-analysis; high confidence]
- **The flagship "brief writing" and "reappraisal" findings are replication-fragile.** Ramirez & Beilock (2011, *Science*) reported a large benefit (especially for high-anxious students), but Camerer et al. (2018, *Nature Human Behaviour* 2:637–644) — which reported "a significant effect in the same direction as the original study for 13 (62%) studies, and the effect size of the replications is on average about 50% of the original effect size" — failed to replicate the Ramirez & Beilock experiment twice with well-powered samples (effect slightly negative). Jamieson et al. (2010) found reappraisal raised GRE-quant scores up to 3 months later (small n). Jamieson et al. (2016, *Social Psychological and Personality Science* 7(6):579–587): "Ninety-three students (across five semesters) enrolled in a community college developmental mathematics course were randomly assigned to stress reappraisal or placebo control conditions"; "Reappraisal students reported less math evaluation anxiety and exhibited improved math exam performance relative to controls" (d ≈ 0.53). But the 2026 multi-institution replication (Thormodsæter et al. 2026, *CBE—Life Sciences Education* 25:ar9, published online 23 Feb 2026), a reappraisal replication "across 12 courses at seven higher education institutions," found "no significant difference between the reappraisal and placebo groups" on postintervention trait anxiety (estimate = 0.15, p = 0.52) or state anxiety (estimate = 0.10, p = 0.55), and likewise no effect on exam/course performance (total z-score estimate = 0.09, p = 0.18) — and **no moderation by gender, race, or institution type**. [objective-score; RCT + replication; high confidence that effects are unreliable]
- **Moderation by trait anxiety is the live hypothesis but is itself unreplicated.** Ramirez & Beilock claimed benefits concentrated in habitually test-anxious students; the 2026 replication explicitly did *not* find such concentration ("Regardless of gender, race/ethnicity, or generation in college, the intervention failed to reduce levels of test anxiety or enhance exam performance"). So "helps the high-anxious" is plausible but not bankable. [mixed; moderate-low confidence]
- **Paced breathing reliably lowers *self-reported* state anxiety and raises HRV, but performance transfer is unestablished.** Multiple RCTs show slow-paced breathing (≈4s in/6s out) reduces STAI-state and increases vagally-mediated HRV; none robustly demonstrate objective-score gains. [self-report; RCT; moderate confidence on anxiety, low on performance]
- **The 2026 replication *did* confirm the correlational backdrop:** pre-intervention trait test anxiety negatively predicted exam score (estimate ≈ −0.19, p < .001). The construct matters; the brief fix is what fails. [objective-score; high confidence]

## Details

### Section A — Feature designs

| Feature | Source intervention & evidence (tag) | Dose & placement | App fidelity / honest caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(a) Exam-pressure simulator** (authentic ~2.6 min/item pacing, scored stakes, on-screen countdown, no-pause, no answer reveal) | Graded-exposure / stress-inoculation *application* phase (Meichenbaum & Deffenbacher 1988); transfer-appropriate processing and the GRE speededness construct. Note: **not** directly validated as a test-anxiety treatment on objective scores. [theory + construct-coverage; moderate confidence] | Opt-in mode; 1–2 full-/half-length timed sets per week in the final 4–6 weeks; mirrors real section length/timing. | High fidelity to authentic test conditions; honest framing: justified primarily because performance-under-time *is* the construct, not because exposure is a proven anxiety cure. |
| **(b) Pre-session arousal-reappraisal microcopy** ("a racing heart and nerves are your body mobilizing resources—this arousal can *help* performance") | Jamieson et al. (2010, GRE; 2016, college math, d ≈ 0.53). [objective-score; RCT; replication-fragile—Thormodsæter et al. 2026 null] | One 2–4 sentence card before timed/simulator sessions; ~15 seconds. | Trivial to build; near-zero cost/risk. Fidelity high to Jamieson wording. Do not over-claim: expected effect small/uncertain. |
| **(c) Worry-dump (expressive writing)** — 2–3 min free-write about exam worries before a high-stakes session | Ramirez & Beilock (2011, *Science*). [objective-score; RCT; **failed replication** Camerer et al. 2018] | Once, immediately before a simulator/mock session; optional, skippable. | Easy to build (text box, not stored/scored). Fidelity high. Caveat: the single most replication-failed component—frame as optional, not a headline. |
| **(d) Optional paced-breathing downregulation** (4s in / 6s out, ~5 min) | Slow-paced-breathing RCTs (reduce STAI-state, raise HRV). [self-report; RCT; performance transfer unproven] | Optional pre-session; user-initiated. | Easy to build. Honest: lowers felt anxiety, but no evidence it raises scores; keep as comfort feature, not performance claim. |
| **(e) Difficulty calibrated under the timer** | Desirable-difficulty / learning-vs-performance (Soderstrom & Bjork 2015): training under representative conditions. [theory; moderate confidence] | Adaptive item selection within timed mode. | Reuses existing item bank; fidelity to construct high. |

### Section B — Measurement plan
- **Primary outcome (objective, under pressure):** raw score on a **timed, no-pause held-out item set** drawn from the existing leakage-isolated, expert-rated bank (reserve one of the four partitions for the delayed endpoint so it is never seen during practice). Administer at a **delayed endpoint** (≥1 week after the intervention block) to capture learning rather than transient performance (Soderstrom & Bjork 2015). Timing fixed at the GRE rate so "performance under pressure" is in-construct.
- **Secondary / mechanism (self-report):** **STAI-state** (Spielberger 1970/1983; 20 items, score 20–80) administered *immediately after* the timed set as a state-anxiety snapshot; **Cognitive Test Anxiety Scale** (Cassady & Johnson 2002; 27 items, range 27–108) at **baseline only** as the trait/stratification variable (the cognitive-worry facet is the most performance-relevant).
- **Anti-demand safeguards:** (1) collect state anxiety *after* performance and frame it as a routine "how did that session feel" check, never as the headline outcome; (2) use a neutral cover story for arm assignment; (3) do not tell participants the feature is hypothesized to reduce anxiety; (4) pre-register that self-report is secondary and that a self-report-only win is treated as a null on the primary; (5) collect CTAS at intake before any feature exposure.

### Section C — Ablation protocol
- **Arms:** (1) full app **with** anxiety bundle on; (2) app with anxiety bundle **off** (everything else identical); (3) **plain Anki** (no interleaving, no anxiety bundle) as an external reference. To keep the ablation clean, hold interleaving constant across arms 1–2 so the contrast isolates the anxiety bundle.
- **Design at tiny n:** prefer **within-subject / crossover** on alternating item partitions (each learner does a timed held-out set under feature-on and feature-off conditions, order counterbalanced) to maximize power; report individual-level estimates. Hold constant: item-partition difficulty, total study time, timing rate, interleaving schedule, and number of timed sessions.
- **Honest power statement:** detecting dz ≈ 0.3 needs ~90 learners; the best meta-analytic performance estimate for test-anxiety interventions is g ≈ 0.28 (Huntley et al. 2019, outliers removed) — i.e., the expected effect sits right at the threshold the pilot cannot detect. **The pilot is therefore an estimation/feasibility study, not a significance test.**
- **TOST equivalence bounds (raw units):** pre-register a smallest effect size of interest, e.g. **±2 raw items on a 20-item timed held-out set** (≈ dz 0.3–0.4 at typical SDs), and use two one-sided tests (Lakens 2017) to ask whether the feature-on vs feature-off difference falls **within** ±2 items (practical equivalence / honest null) or whether the 90% CI excludes the bound.
- **Trait-anxiety stratification:** pre-specify the **high-CTAS subgroup** (top tertile, Cassady & Johnson 2002) as the subgroup in which any benefit is expected to concentrate; report it as exploratory given power.
- **Pre-registered one-sentence hypothesis:** *"Among GRE-math learners, turning the anxiety bundle on (vs off) will change the delayed, timed held-out raw score by an amount whose 90% CI we will compare against a pre-set equivalence bound of ±2 items, with any positive effect expected to be larger in the high-CTAS subgroup."*
- **Honest-null template:** *"At n = X, the feature-on vs feature-off difference on the delayed timed set was [Δ items, 90% CI]. This interval fell within / overlapped our ±2-item equivalence bound, so we [cannot distinguish the feature from no effect / report a practically equivalent null]. The high-CTAS subgroup estimate was [Δ], directionally [consistent/inconsistent] but underpowered."*

### Section D — Risk table

| Risk | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Iatrogenic anxiety | Cueing/worry-dumping can amplify rumination for some learners (esp. low-anxious); reappraisal framing may backfire | Make worry-dump optional/skippable; default reappraisal microcopy brief and forward-looking; monitor STAI-state for increases; allow opt-out |
| Placebo / demand effects | Self-reported calm inflates apparent success | Primary outcome is objective score; STAI-state collected after performance, framed neutrally; pre-register self-report as secondary |
| Opportunity cost | Time on anxiety features displaces content study and interleaving | Cap bundle at minutes/session; keep adjunct to (not replacing) timed practice; track total study time |
| Moderation / null-or-negative for low-anxious | Benefits (if any) concentrate in high-worry students; Yerkes-Dodson implies arousal can help on moderate-difficulty tasks | Stratify by CTAS; consider gating worry-dump to higher-anxious users; report subgroup transparently |
| Over-claiming from a noisy pilot | Tiny-n false positives; effect-size inflation (Camerer et al. 2018: replications ~50% of original) | TOST + estimation framing; pre-registration; honest-null template; no "it works" language without replication |

### Section E — The decision (evidence-tagged)
- **REPLACE interleaving — rejected.** Interleaving has its own (also math-attenuated) evidence base and already holds the slot; the anxiety-intervention performance evidence is weaker and more replication-fragile (Camerer et al. 2018 failed replication; Thormodsæter et al. 2026 null; Huntley g = 0.28 with outliers removed). No basis to displace. [objective-score; meta-analysis + replication; high confidence]
- **COMPLEMENT — recommended.** Ship the low-cost components (reappraisal microcopy, optional worry-dump, optional breathing) as **un-ablated defaults**, and build the exam-pressure simulator justified by the **speededness construct** (Soderstrom & Bjork 2015; transfer-appropriate processing) rather than the anxiety literature. Keep interleaving as the single pre-registered ablation to protect power; if you must ablate the anxiety bundle, run it as a clearly-labeled secondary estimation pilot with TOST, accepting it is underpowered. [theory + construct-coverage; high confidence this is the prudent allocation]
- **DEPRIORITIZE (as the ablation slot) — adopt this for the slot.** Do not spend the scarce, power-limited ablation on a feature whose expected effect (g ≈ 0.28) sits below the detectable threshold (dz ≈ 0.3 needs ~90). Two simultaneous ablated features under tiny n would split power and produce two uninterpretable nulls — confront this honestly rather than promising both.
- **Falsifiable flip condition:** elevate the anxiety bundle to a **co-primary feature** if, in the **high-CTAS (top-tertile) subgroup**, the feature-on vs feature-off delayed timed-set difference **exceeds the +2-item equivalence bound (dz ≥ ~0.3) and the 90% CI excludes zero** in a replication or adequately powered follow-up. Conversely, a within-bound (equivalent-to-null) result in that subgroup confirms deprioritization.

## Recommendations
1. **Keep interleaving as the required learning-science feature + ablation.** Do not let anxiety inoculation consume the slot.
2. **Ship the cheap anxiety components now as defaults** (reappraisal microcopy; optional worry-dump; optional paced breathing) — near-zero cost, low risk, mild upside, no performance over-claim.
3. **Build the exam-pressure simulator** and justify it on speededness/construct grounds; this is the most defensible "feature" because it doesn't depend on the fragile anxiety literature.
4. **If you ablate the anxiety bundle at all,** run it as a *secondary*, pre-registered TOST estimation pilot (within-subject, delayed timed held-out endpoint, ±2-item equivalence bound), reported with the honest-null template and CTAS stratification.
5. **Thresholds that change the call:** flip to co-primary if the high-CTAS subgroup shows a >2-item, CI-excludes-zero benefit; abandon the worry-dump if STAI-state rises or the estimate is negative; keep breathing only as a comfort feature unless objective scores move.

## Caveats
- All "performance" claims for brief anxiety interventions rest on a literature with **documented replication failures and effect-size inflation** (Camerer et al. 2018; Thormodsæter et al. 2026); treat single positive studies (Ramirez & Beilock 2011; Jamieson et al. 2010, 2016) as suggestive, not settled.
- The learning-science base is weaker in **math** than verbal, compounding the modest expected effect.
- At tiny n, **the deliverable is the design and an honest estimation/equivalence result, not a significant effect.**
- Moderation by trait anxiety is plausible but **itself unreplicated**; do not pre-commit to it as fact.
- Note a within-literature tension: anxiety *reduction* is reliably achievable (Ergene E++ = 0.65; Huntley g = −0.64) while *performance* barely moves (g = 0.28) — a self-report win is not a score win, which is exactly why the primary outcome must be objective.

## Self-confidence
High confidence in the directional read (interleaving retains the slot; anxiety inoculation is a low-cost complement with weak/fragile objective-score evidence). Moderate confidence in the specific TOST bound (±2 items is a reasonable but adjustable SESOI). **One-line recommendation: COMPLEMENT (deprioritized relative to interleaving for the ablation slot) — evidence tag: [objective-score meta-analysis g ≈ 0.28 + failed replications: Camerer et al. 2018, Thormodsæter et al. 2026].**

## Sources

**Meta-analyses**
- von der Embse, N. P., Jester, D., Roy, D., & Post, J. (2018). Test anxiety effects, predictors, and correlates: A 30-year meta-analytic review. *Journal of Affective Disorders*, 227, 483–493. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jad.2017.11.048
- Hembree, R. (1988). Correlates, causes, effects, and treatment of test anxiety. *Review of Educational Research*, 58(1), 47–77. https://doi.org/10.3102/00346543058001047
- Ergene, T. (2003). Effective interventions on test anxiety reduction: A meta-analysis. *School Psychology International*, 24(3), 313–328. https://doi.org/10.1177/01430343030243004
- Huntley, C. D., Young, B., Temple, J., Longworth, M., Tudur Smith, C., Jha, V., & Fisher, P. L. (2019). The efficacy of interventions for test-anxious university students: A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. *Journal of Anxiety Disorders*, 63, 36–50. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.janxdis.2019.01.007

**RCTs / empirical (objective-score and replications)**
- Ramirez, G., & Beilock, S. L. (2011). Writing about testing worries boosts exam performance in the classroom. *Science*, 331(6014), 211–213. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1199427
- Camerer, C. F., et al. (2018). Evaluating the replicability of social science experiments in Nature and Science between 2010 and 2015. *Nature Human Behaviour*, 2, 637–644. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-018-0399-z
- Jamieson, J. P., Mendes, W. B., Blackstock, E., & Schmader, T. (2010). Turning the knots in your stomach into bows: Reappraising arousal improves performance on the GRE. *Journal of Experimental Social Psychology*, 46(1), 208–212. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2009.08.015
- Jamieson, J. P., Peters, B. J., Greenwood, E. J., & Altose, A. J. (2016). Reappraising stress arousal improves performance and reduces evaluation anxiety in classroom exam situations. *Social Psychological and Personality Science*, 7(6), 579–587. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550616644656
- Thormodsæter, R., Ballen, C., Fagbodun, S., Foster-Hartnett, D., García-Ojeda, M., Gonsar, N., Henning, J., Robnett, R., Sanders, T., Wainman, J., Costello, R., Cotner, S., & Schussler, E. (2026). Can we mitigate the impacts of test anxiety through reappraisal interventions? A replication study in science courses across multiple institution types in the United States. *CBE—Life Sciences Education*, 25(1), ar9. https://doi.org/10.1187/cbe.25-04-0055

**Measures / constructs**
- Cassady, J. C., & Johnson, R. E. (2002). Cognitive test anxiety and academic performance. *Contemporary Educational Psychology*, 27(2), 270–295. https://doi.org/10.1006/ceps.2001.1094
- Spielberger, C. D., Gorsuch, R. L., & Lushene, R. E. (1970; 1983). *Manual for the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI)*. Palo Alto, CA: Consulting Psychologists Press.

**Theory / methods**
- Meichenbaum, D. H., & Deffenbacher, J. L. (1988). Stress inoculation training. *The Counseling Psychologist*, 16(1), 69–90. https://doi.org/10.1177/0011000088161005
- Soderstrom, N. C., & Bjork, R. A. (2015). Learning versus performance: An integrative review. *Perspectives on Psychological Science*, 10(2), 176–199. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691615569000
- Lakens, D. (2017). Equivalence tests: A practical primer for t tests, correlations, and meta-analyses. *Social Psychological and Personality Science*, 8(4), 355–362. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550617697177
